text: The spinal cord is a cylindrical bundle of nerve fibers and associated tissue enclosed within the vertebral column. It extends from the medulla oblongata to approximately the L1-L2 vertebral level in adults. The spinal cord contains 31 pairs of spinal nerves, each with a dorsal (sensory) root and a ventral (motor) root.

Each spinal nerve divides shortly after leaving the vertebral column into dorsal and ventral rami. The ventral (anterior) rami of spinal nerves C5 through T1 form the brachial plexus. The ventral rami are larger than the dorsal rami and supply the limbs and the anterolateral trunk.

The brachial plexus is a complex network of nerve fibers that supplies the upper extremity. It arises from the neck and passes through the axilla to distribute branches to the shoulder, arm, forearm, and hand. The plexus lies posterior to the clavicle and anterior to the serratus anterior muscle.

The brachial plexus is formed by the anterior (ventral) rami of spinal nerves C5, C6, C7, C8, and T1. A small contribution from C4 may occur (prefixed plexus) and occasionally from T2 (postfixed). These five roots are the foundation from which all terminal branches of the arm ultimately originate.

After the five roots of the brachial plexus emerge from between the anterior and middle scalene muscles, they reorganize into trunks. This reorganization marks the transition from root-level anatomy to trunk-level anatomy, which is important for understanding nerve injury localization.

The five roots of the brachial plexus converge to form three trunks: (1) The upper trunk is formed by the union of C5 and C6 roots. (2) The middle trunk is a continuation of the C7 root alone. (3) The lower trunk is formed by the union of C8 and T1 roots. Erb's point is where C5 and C6 unite; injury here causes Erb's palsy.

Each of the three trunks of the brachial plexus divides into an anterior and a posterior division, yielding six divisions total. This division is significant because it determines whether fibers will eventually supply flexor (anterior division) or extensor (posterior division) compartments of the upper limb.

Each trunk divides into anterior and posterior divisions. The anterior divisions supply the flexor compartments; posterior divisions supply the extensor compartments. The posterior divisions of all three trunks unite to form the posterior cord. The anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks form the lateral cord, while the anterior division of the lower trunk forms the medial cord.

The divisions of the brachial plexus regroup to form three cords — lateral, medial, and posterior — named according to their relationship to the axillary artery. This relationship to the axillary artery is the anatomical landmark used to identify cord positions on imaging and in surgery.

Three cords are formed from the divisions: (1) Lateral cord (C5-C7): from anterior divisions of upper and middle trunks. Gives rise to the musculocutaneous nerve and the lateral head of the median nerve. (2) Medial cord (C8-T1): from anterior division of lower trunk. Gives rise to the ulnar nerve and the medial head of the median nerve. (3) Posterior cord (C5-T1): from all three posterior divisions. Gives rise to the axillary nerve and radial nerve.

The terminal branches of the brachial plexus are the named peripheral nerves that distribute to specific muscles and skin areas of the upper extremity. Each terminal nerve carries fibers from multiple spinal levels, which is why single-nerve lesions produce characteristic but often partial sensory and motor deficits.

The five major terminal branches of the brachial plexus are: (1) Musculocutaneous nerve (C5-C7) — from lateral cord, supplies biceps brachii and brachialis (elbow flexion). (2) Median nerve (C5-T1) — from both lateral and medial cords, supplies most forearm flexors and thenar muscles. (3) Ulnar nerve (C8-T1) — from medial cord, supplies intrinsic hand muscles and hypothenar muscles. (4) Radial nerve (C5-T1) — from posterior cord, supplies all extensors of upper limb. (5) Axillary nerve (C5-C6) — from posterior cord, supplies deltoid and teres minor.

The axillary nerve leaves the brachial plexus at the axilla and passes through the quadrilateral space (bounded by the teres major, teres minor, long head of triceps, and humerus). Dislocation of the shoulder joint or fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus can stretch or tear the axillary nerve.

The axillary nerve (C5-C6) arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus. It innervates: (1) Deltoid muscle — responsible for shoulder abduction (especially 15-90°), flexion, and extension. (2) Teres minor — external rotation of the shoulder. (3) Skin over the lateral arm (regimental badge area). Injury causes: inability to abduct the shoulder past 15°, loss of sensation over lateral arm, flat shoulder appearance due to deltoid atrophy.

The radial nerve is the largest branch of the brachial plexus and the only one to supply the extensor compartments throughout the entire upper limb. It spirals around the posterior humerus in the radial (spiral) groove before piercing the lateral intermuscular septum to enter the anterior compartment of the arm.

The radial nerve (C5-T1) arises from the posterior cord. It innervates all extensors of the arm, forearm, and wrist: triceps brachii, brachioradialis, extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis, and via the posterior interosseous nerve (deep branch), all other extensor muscles of the forearm. Injury in the spiral groove causes wrist drop (inability to extend wrist), finger drop, and sensory loss over dorsal hand. Triceps function is spared with mid-shaft humeral fracture.

The median nerve passes through the antecubital fossa medial to the brachial artery, enters the forearm between the two heads of pronator teres, and descends through the forearm in the plane between flexor digitorum superficialis and profundus. It enters the hand through the carpal tunnel beneath the flexor retinaculum.

The median nerve (C5-T1) arises from both the lateral and medial cords. It supplies: forearm pronators (pronator teres, pronator quadratus), most wrist and finger flexors (except flexor carpi ulnaris and medial two FDP), thenar muscles (opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis, superficial head of flexor pollicis brevis), and first two lumbricals. Injury at wrist (carpal tunnel syndrome) causes Ape hand deformity: thenar wasting, loss of thumb opposition, and sensory loss over lateral 3.5 fingers.

The ulnar nerve runs medially through the arm without giving off any branches, passes posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus (the 'funny bone' groove), and enters the forearm between the two heads of flexor carpi ulnaris. It crosses the wrist superficially via Guyon's canal.

The ulnar nerve (C8-T1) arises from the medial cord. It supplies: flexor carpi ulnaris, medial two heads of flexor digitorum profundus, all intrinsic hand muscles (interossei, hypothenar muscles, medial two lumbricals), and adductor pollicis. Injury at elbow causes claw hand deformity (more prominent in ring/little fingers), loss of finger abduction/adduction, and sensory loss over medial 1.5 fingers and medial palm.

The rotator cuff is a group of four muscles that surround the glenohumeral joint, forming a cuff of tendons that blend with the joint capsule. These muscles are collectively responsible for stabilizing the humeral head against the glenoid fossa, preventing superior migration during deltoid contraction.

The four rotator cuff muscles (mnemonic SITS): (1) Supraspinatus — superior part of cuff, initiates abduction (first 15°), innervated by suprascapular nerve (C5-C6). (2) Infraspinatus — posterior, external rotation, suprascapular nerve (C5-C6). (3) Teres Minor — posterior, external rotation, axillary nerve (C5-C6). (4) Subscapularis — anterior, internal rotation and adduction, subscapular nerves (C5-C6). The supraspinatus tendon is most commonly torn (insertion on greater tubercle of humerus).

The supraspinatus originates from the supraspinous fossa of the scapula and inserts on the superior facet of the greater tubercle of the humerus. It initiates shoulder abduction for the first 15° before the deltoid takes over. It is innervated by the suprascapular nerve (C5-C6). The supraspinatus is tested clinically with the empty-can test (arm at 90° abduction, 30° horizontal adduction, internally rotated). It is the most commonly torn rotator cuff muscle.

The infraspinatus originates from the infraspinous fossa of the scapula and inserts on the middle facet of the greater tubercle of the humerus. Its primary action is external rotation of the shoulder; it also assists with horizontal abduction. Innervation: suprascapular nerve (C5-C6). Clinical test: resist external rotation with elbow at 90° (external rotation lag sign for full-thickness tears).

The subscapularis originates from the subscapular fossa (anterior surface of scapula) and inserts on the lesser tubercle of the humerus. It is the only rotator cuff muscle on the anterior surface of the scapula. Primary actions: internal rotation and adduction of the shoulder. Innervation: upper and lower subscapular nerves (C5-C6), branches of the posterior cord. Clinical test: lift-off test (arm internally rotated behind back, lift away from body).
